title: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis
summary: The checked-in XML is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item deathjas, a 2003 encoding of an anonymous Glasgow reset printed in Miscellanea Scotica, volume II (1818). That reset reproduces John Pinkerton's first publication in The History of Scotland, volume I (1797), Appendix XIII, which directly transcribes the defective witness British Library Add MS 5467, ff. 72v-84v and fragment f. 72*. The other complete witnesses, British Library Add MS 38690 and National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.17.1.22, are collateral witnesses rather than ancestors of the encoded text. George Neilson's 1904 article prints material from the NLS copy, while Lister M. Matheson's 1999 edition uses Add MS 38690 as base text and collates Add MS 5467. No public full facsimile of any of the three manuscripts was verified on 2026-07-15; the repository catalogs, onsite consultation, and reproduction-order routes are recorded instead. Current U-M No Copyright-United States labeling conflicts with the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML, and image-reuse terms for the cached third-party scans remain source-specific.
review_notes: The lost Latin narrative is an intellectual source named by the Shirley colophon, not a surviving physical witness; its author, title, exemplar, and transmission remain unknown.
The three manuscript witnesses are represented independently. No direct witness-to-witness relation or stemma is asserted.
The title page's MCCCCXL claim is retained as an 1818/Pinkerton editorial dating claim, not converted into a demonstrated exact date for the translation or Add MS 5467.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for deathjas
entities.description: The exact XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The pinned file is 43,063 bytes.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item deathjas
entities.description: A 2003 electronic text of the 1818 title page, anonymous Advertisement, and chronicle on printed pages 5-29. It retains Pinkerton-derived editorial notes and the Add MS 5467 lacuna, but its header compresses the intervening Pinkerton and 1818 print layers into a direct manuscript-source statement.
notes: The PROFILEDESC declares Latin, but the encoded work is predominantly Middle English and includes one brief Middle French maxim; Latin belongs to the lost source relationship rather than the encoded language inventory.
The BODY contains 31 prose paragraphs and one four-line closing stanza.

entities.label: The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis (abstract Middle English work)
alternate_labels: A Full Lamentable Cronycle of the Dethe and False Murdure of James Stewarde, Last Kynge of Scotys
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English prose account of James I of Scotland's murder in 1437 and the punishment of the conspirators, translated from a now-lost Latin narrative by John Shirley and distinguished here from its witnesses, editions, encodings, and print paratexts.

entities.label: Anonymous Glasgow reset in Miscellanea Scotica, volume II (1818)
entities.description: The immediate printed source of the U-M encoding: a reset of Pinkerton's Appendix XIII with a new title page and anonymous Advertisement. The Advertisement says the tract first appeared in Pinkerton and disputes his claim that the lost Latin account was published in Scotland by authority.

bibliographic.citation: The Dethe of James Kynge of Scotis, in Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland, vol. II (Glasgow: printed for and sold by John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer, 1818), separately paginated pp. 1-29.
bibliographic.title: Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland
bibliographic.publisher: John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer
bibliographic.pages: separately paginated pp. 1-29; historical text pp. 5-29

notes: The title leaf's manuscript date MCCCCXL repeats Pinkerton's inference from a date attached to another Shirley translation; it is not treated as a demonstrated date for Add MS 5467 or for the translation.

entities.label: Internet Archive delivery of the Google/University of Michigan scan of Miscellanea Scotica, volume II (1818)
entities.description: A complete 401-page PDF scan plus OCR and page-structure derivatives. The target title is at PDF page 275 / BookReader n274, the Advertisement at PDF page 277 / n276, and the historical text at PDF pages 279-303 / n278-n302.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete PDF page-image scan with OCR and scandata derivatives

entities.label: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland, volume I (1797), Appendix XIII
entities.description: The first identified publication of the chronicle, printed as Appendix XIII on pages 462-475. Pinkerton explicitly identifies his source as British Library Add MS 5467, marks the torn-leaf gap, reports the Shirley colophon, and adds historical and lexical notes.

bibliographic.citation: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers, vol. I (London: C. Dilly, 1797), Appendix XIII, pp. 462-475.
bibliographic.title: The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers

entities.label: Internet Archive delivery of the Digital Library of India scan of Pinkerton's History of Scotland, volume I (1797)
entities.description: A complete 538-page PDF scan plus OCR and page-structure derivatives. Appendix XIII occupies printed pages 462-475, PDF pages 479-492, or BookReader n478-n491.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete PDF page-image scan with OCR and scandata derivatives

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 5467, ff. 72v-84v and fragment f. 72*
entities.description: The defective manuscript witness directly transcribed by Pinkerton. A torn leaf between ff. 72 and 73 causes the gap inherited by the 1797 publication, 1818 reset, U-M encoding, and repository artifact; a fragment numbered f. 72* preserves part of the lost leaf.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: Mid-to-late fifteenth century; the composite volume's internal reference to Henry VI as formerly king suggests part was copied after 1461
date_statements.notes: The catalog's composite-volume range and internal clue do not establish an exact date for this item's copying.

holding.notes: The chronicle is item 6 at ff. 72v-84v, with fragment f. 72*.

physical_description.extent: 224 folios plus fragment f. 72*; chronicle on ff. 72v-84v
notes: The composite manuscript contains several texts and hands.
The catalog tentatively associates the hand across ff. 23-224 with Rouland Brugge; this is not converted into certain scribal attribution.
The closing colophon identifies John Shirley as translator from Latin in his last age.
No public full digital facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 38690, ff. 9-15
entities.description: A complete early witness of the chronicle, now cataloged circa 1440. It preserves the passage missing from Add MS 5467 and serves as the base text of Matheson's 1999 edition.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: A cataloged approximate witness date, not proof that translation and copying were simultaneous or that this is Shirley's autograph.

holding.notes: The chronicle is item 4 at ff. 9-15.

physical_description.support: Paper manuscript formerly configured as a roll
physical_description.format: Formerly a roll 22 ft 5 in long
notes: The present codex format postdates the original roll configuration.
No public full digital facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.17.1.22, ff. 13-25
entities.description: A complete later copy of the chronicle. George Neilson used ff. 14-15 to print the passage missing from Add MS 5467 and also printed fuller concluding material; its date remains disputed between the current NLS sixteenth-century cataloging and Neilson's description of an unidentified seventeenth-century hand.

date_statements.label: Current repository date
date_statements.notes: The current browse record conflicts with Neilson's 1904 description of a seventeenth-century hand.

date_statements.label: Earlier scholarly assessment
date_statements.notes: Recorded as a historical assessment, not preferred over the current repository date without further paleographical review.

holding.notes: Neilson locates the complete chronicle at ff. 13-25 and the missing Add MS 5467 passage principally at ff. 14-15.

notes: No public full digital facsimile was verified on 2026-07-15.
No direct copying relation to either British Library witness is asserted.

entities.label: George Neilson, Missing Section of The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis, Recovered (1904)
entities.description: A short scholarly article transcribing the passage absent from Add MS 5467 from NLS Adv.MS.17.1.22, ff. 14-15, and printing additional concluding readings from the NLS copy.

bibliographic.citation: George Neilson, Missing Section of The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis, Recovered, Scottish Historical Review 2, no. 1 (October 1904): 97-99.
bibliographic.title: Missing Section of The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis, Recovered

entities.label: Electric Scotland PDF of Scottish Historical Review 2, no. 1 (1904)
entities.description: A complete 118-page issue PDF. Neilson's article occupies printed pages 97-99, corresponding to PDF pages 102-104.

entities.label: Lister M. Matheson, Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles (1999), part I
entities.description: A modern scholarly edition whose contents identify the text as the text of BL Add MS 38690 collated with BL Add MS 5467. It includes separate discussions of previous editions, manuscripts, Shirley, recipient, source, and treatment.

bibliographic.citation: Lister M. Matheson, ed., Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles (Woodbridge: Boydell Press, 1999), part I.
bibliographic.title: Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles

notes: The publicly inspected contents sample establishes the base/collation statement but is not a substitute for consulting the full edition's manuscript and source arguments.

rights.component: The medieval Middle English chronicle as an abstract textual work
rights.basis: The work was translated and copied in the fifteenth century; no modern authorship is involved in the underlying medieval text.

rights.component: Pinkerton's 1797 published edition and editorial notes
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1797 and John Pinkerton died in 1826; ordinary copyright terms expired long before 2026.

rights.component: The anonymous 1818 reset, title page, Advertisement, and published typography
rights.basis: The anonymous edition was published in 1818; ordinary copyright and edition-layout terms expired long before 2026.

rights.component: The current U-M digital item and encoding in the United States
rights.basis: The current U-M item presents the source as public domain and displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright-United States designation.
notes: The embedded 2003 availability statement separately forbids redistribution by commercial and not-for-profit content providers. The two dated provider layers are preserved rather than silently reconciled.
The NoC-US designation is territorially limited to the United States.

rights.component: The redistribution terms embedded in the 2003 XML
rights.basis: The XML allows personal use, research, teaching, and linking with attribution but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
notes: This is a provider-term layer, not proof that U-M owns copyright in the medieval text or nineteenth-century editions.

rights.component: The checked-in XML artifact, including encoding structure and copied provider statement
rights.basis: The artifact carries the U-M legacy availability text, while the current U-M item carries NoC-US. This record does not infer a separate repository grant or ownership conclusion from those conflicting layers.

rights.component: The Google/University of Michigan scan and Internet Archive-delivered derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying 1818 edition is public domain, but the scan front matter requests noncommercial use and attribution and restricts automated querying; no broader item-specific reuse license was verified.
notes: The cached files are retained as private research evidence; public-domain status of the book is not treated as automatic permission under every provider term.

rights.component: The Digital Library of India / Internet Archive scan and derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying 1797 edition is public domain, but no item-specific license or rights statement for the delivered scan was found in the inspected Internet Archive item metadata.

rights.component: The Electric Scotland complete-issue PDF scan
rights.basis: The PDF is publicly downloadable, but no item-specific reuse license for the scan was verified on the inspected source page.

rights.component: Any images supplied by the National Library of Scotland on request
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms and applicable copyright law
rights.basis: NLS states that copying depends on condition and copyright review and that supplied-item terms must be checked. Its general CC0 position for faithful digitizations of out-of-copyright material is not extended to an image set that has not been supplied or licensed.

rights.component: Matheson's modern edited text, introduction, apparatus, and book design
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom, United States, and other applicable territories
rights.basis: The modern scholarly edition was published in 1999 and remains commercially available; no open license was displayed on the inspected publisher page or contents sample.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Add MS 5467 has a torn leaf between ff. 72 and 73 and preserves only a fragment now numbered f. 72*.
editorial_practices.representation: Pinkerton directly transcribes the defective witness, explicitly marks the missing-leaf interruption, and adds footnotes on the manuscript, Shirley's Latin-source colophon, readings, language, and historical interpretation.
editorial_practices.description: The gap and editorial notes are inherited by the 1818 reset and U-M encoding; they belong to the Add MS 5467/Pinkerton transmission rather than the complete abstract work.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Pinkerton's Appendix XIII appears within a two-volume historical work and includes his prefatory and footnote framing.
editorial_practices.representation: The 1818 printer resets the complete Pinkerton-derived chronicle as a separately titled tract, preserves the lacuna and notes, and adds an anonymous Advertisement disputing one of Pinkerton's source claims.
editorial_practices.description: The 1818 title page and Advertisement are print paratexts, not parts of the medieval work or evidence of a new manuscript source.
editorial_practices.scope: The separately paginated 1818 tract within Miscellanea Scotica, volume II

editorial_practices.source_feature: Miscellanea Scotica, volume II is a 401-page multi-tract volume; the target tract has its own title, Advertisement, and pages 1-29.
editorial_practices.representation: The U-M item encodes only the target title page, Advertisement, and historical text, omitting the volume's surrounding tracts and volume-level matter.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding is complete for its selected defective print version but selective relative to the 1818 carrier volume and incomplete relative to complete manuscript witnesses.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The actual source chain is Add MS 5467 to Pinkerton 1797 to the anonymous 1818 reset to the U-M encoding.
editorial_practices.representation: The XML source description names Add MS 5467 as though the 1818 source were a direct manuscript edition, while exact typography and the 1818 Advertisement show two intervening print layers.
editorial_practices.description: Researchers should use the explicit relation chain rather than infer direct U-M consultation of Add MS 5467.
editorial_practices.scope: U-M HEADER/SOURCEDESC and encoded title, notes, and page milestones

editorial_practices.source_feature: The 1818 tract's historical text is correctly paginated 5-29.
editorial_practices.representation: The XML has 25 BODY page-break milestones but repeats p. 26 where the scan shows the second milestone should be p. 27.
editorial_practices.description: The error is an encoding-level page-number transcription fault and does not imply a repeated or missing printed page.
editorial_practices.scope: U-M BODY/PB sequence near the end of the encoded text

editorial_practices.source_feature: Add MS 5467 lacks a passage because of a torn leaf, whereas Adv.MS.17.1.22 preserves a complete later copy and a fuller conclusion.
editorial_practices.representation: Neilson transcribes the recovered NLS passage and fuller concluding readings and explains how the later witness supplements Pinkerton's defective source.
editorial_practices.description: Neilson is collateral evidence for missing content; his article is not an ancestor of the U-M encoding.
editorial_practices.scope: Neilson 1904, printed pp. 97-99, especially NLS ff. 14-15

editorial_practices.source_feature: Add MS 38690 is complete while Add MS 5467 is defective but preserves the Shirley translator colophon and a distinct witness text.
editorial_practices.representation: Matheson presents the text of Add MS 38690 collated with Add MS 5467.
editorial_practices.description: The modern edition is a collateral scholarly reconstruction, not the source of the 1818-derived U-M item.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/deathjas.xml, pinned CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: Complete file; especially HEADER, SOURCEDESC, AVAILABILITY, FRONT/TITLEPAGE, FRONT/DIV1, BODY/DIV1, PB, NOTE1, and closing LG
evidence.summary: The 43,063-byte XML identifies AAM1058/deathjas, a 2003 encoding of the 1818 Glasgow tract derived from Add MS 5467. It preserves the title page, anonymous Advertisement, defective Middle English chronicle, French maxim, Pinkerton-derived notes, Shirley translator colophon, and restrictive legacy availability statement.

evidence.citation: Direct XPath, structural, and textual inspection of the pinned deathjas.xml artifact.
evidence.locator: Element counts and FRONT/BODY-scoped counts; BODY PB sequence; language-bearing passages
evidence.summary: The file contains 2 DIV1, 37 P, 1 LG, 4 L, 27 PB, and 8 NOTE1 elements. Four P are in HEADER, two in the FRONT Advertisement, and 31 in the BODY. FRONT has two PB and BODY 25; scan comparison identifies the repeated p. 26 milestone as printed p. 27. The encoded work is Middle English with a brief Middle French maxim, not Latin.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, The dethe of James Kynge of Scotis / translated by John Shirley, CME item deathjas.
evidence.locator: Item information, contents, production context, rights statement, and stable link
evidence.summary: The current item identifies the inherited Shirley author display, Glasgow 1818 publication statement, two-part contents, stable deathjas identifier, public-domain representation, and No Copyright-United States label.
notes: The inherited author display is narrowed to translator in the manifests because the Add MS 5467 colophon identifies Shirley's translation role rather than authorship of the lost Latin narrative.

evidence.citation: Miscellanea Scotica: A Collection of Tracts Relating to the History, Antiquities, Topography, and Literature of Scotland, vol. II (Glasgow: John Wylie & Co.; R. Chapman, printer, 1818), complete Google/University of Michigan scan delivered by Internet Archive.
evidence.locator: Volume title and contents; target title PDF p. 275/n274, Advertisement PDF p. 277/n276, historical text PDF pp. 279-303/n278-n302, printed pp. 5-29
evidence.summary: Complete-volume evidence for the 1818 carrier, John Wylie and Robert Chapman imprints, title and Advertisement, work boundary and page sequence, reset relationship to Pinkerton, Google provider notice, and the direct typographic source of the XML.

evidence.citation: Direct page-image comparison of the cached complete 1818 Miscellanea Scotica volume with deathjas.xml.
evidence.locator: Target title, Advertisement, printed pp. 5-29, footnotes, and page transitions
evidence.summary: Page-image comparison confirms exact source typography and wording, the Pinkerton-derived lacuna and notes, and that the XML's second p. 26 milestone should identify printed p. 27.

evidence.citation: John Pinkerton, The History of Scotland from the Accession of the House of Stuart to That of Mary, with Appendixes of Original Papers, vol. I (London: C. Dilly, 1797), Appendix XIII, pp. 462-475.
evidence.locator: Printed pp. 462-475; PDF pp. 479-492; BookReader n478-n491
evidence.summary: First-publication evidence for Pinkerton's direct Add MS 5467 transcription, explicit torn-leaf gap, Shirley translator/source note, manuscript provenance, inherited circa-1440 inference, editorial notes, and the text later reset in 1818.

evidence.citation: Direct page-image comparison of cached Pinkerton 1797 Appendix XIII with the 1818 reset and deathjas.xml.
evidence.locator: Appendix XIII heading, manuscript statement, lacuna marker, body text, footnotes, and Shirley colophon
evidence.summary: Comparison confirms that Pinkerton directly names Add MS 5467, that the 1818 tract resets his chronicle and notes, and that the Add MS 5467 defect rather than a U-M omission causes the encoded gap.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 5467, record 032-002029031.
evidence.locator: Collection date and origin; physical description; item 6 at ff. 72v-84v; Digitised Content and access fields
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence for the mid-to-late-fifteenth-century English composite codex, possible Rouland Brugge hand, chronicle locus, torn leaf and fragment f. 72*, Shirley translator colophon, complete Add MS 38690 witness, blank Digitised Content field, and physical-request route.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 38690, record 040-002058005.
evidence.locator: Item 4 at ff. 9-15; circa-1440 date; physical description; Digitised Content and access fields
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence for the complete circa-1440 chronicle witness, its relation to Add MS 5467 and the latter's Shirley colophon, the 18-folio paper object formerly configured as a 22-foot-5-inch roll, blank Digitised Content field, and physical-request route.

evidence.citation: British Library, manuscript consultation and digitisation services.
evidence.locator: Manuscript request, digitisation order, quotation, reference/reproduction image, payment, and permissions guidance
evidence.summary: The current route requires shelfmark, title, and exact folios. Original manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned; reference and reproduction images differ, payment may be required, and publication permission is a separate question.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Complaint made to Henry VI by the Duke of Gloucester, and A fulle lamentable cronicle, Adv.MS.17.1.22, resource 19596.
evidence.locator: Record title, identifier, repository, and date exposed in the current browse result
evidence.summary: Current repository evidence identifying Adv.MS.17.1.22 as the combined Gloucester complaint and James I chronicle file and dating it to the sixteenth century. The item page returned HTTP 403 to the automated client, so no unexposed fields or digital-image claims are inferred.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Consult collections in person.
evidence.locator: Joining, catalog requests, Special Collections Reading Room, and virtual reading-room guidance
evidence.summary: Current access guidance supports in-person consultation after joining and requesting the item, with condition-dependent virtual consultation potentially available by arrangement.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Copying services and copy enquiry form.
evidence.locator: Condition and copyright review, minimum fee, supplied formats, and complete-work copying
evidence.summary: NLS may supply JPEG, TIFF, or PDF copies after condition and copyright review; fees apply, and complete-work scanning depends on collection care and other restrictions.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Copyright guidance.
evidence.locator: Faithful digitization, out-of-copyright materials, CC0, and item-specific responsibilities
evidence.summary: NLS states that faithful digital reproductions of out-of-copyright works generally share the source's copyright status and waives its own rights through CC0 to the extent they subsist, but researchers must still assess item-specific copyright and supplied-copy terms.

evidence.citation: George Neilson, Missing Section of The Dethe of the Kynge of Scotis, Recovered, Scottish Historical Review 2, no. 1 (October 1904): 97-99.
evidence.summary: Neilson states that Pinkerton and Stevenson used Add MS 5467, identifies Adv.MS.17.1.22 as former Phillipps MS 27369 with a full text at ff. 13-25 in what he considered a seventeenth-century hand, and transcribes the missing passage from ff. 14-15 plus fuller concluding material.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection and text extraction of Neilson's article in the cached complete 1904 issue PDF.
evidence.summary: All three article pages are readable and extractable despite recoverable PDF linearization-hint warnings. Direct inspection confirms the NLS folio locations, recovered text, fuller ending, and seventeenth-century-hand assessment.

evidence.citation: Boydell & Brewer, Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles, edited by Lister M. Matheson (1999), ISBN 9780851157252.
evidence.locator: Title, editor, publication date, ISBN, description, availability, and copyright notice
evidence.summary: The publisher describes Shirley's work as a translation from a lost Latin narrative concerning James I's murder and presents the volume as a modern scholarly edition still offered commercially.

evidence.citation: Lister M. Matheson, Death and Dissent: Two Fifteenth-Century Chronicles (1999), public contents sample.
evidence.locator: Three-page contents sample, part I headings and text description
evidence.summary: The contents identify sections on the account, previous editions, manuscripts, Shirley, recipient, source, and treatment, and explicitly describe the edited text as the text of BL MS Additional 38690 collated with BL MS Additional 5467.
notes: The sample does not expose the full edition or its complete stemmatic argument.

evidence.citation: Dated public-facsimile audit for Add MS 5467, Add MS 38690, and Adv.MS.17.1.22.
evidence.locator: Exact shelfmark searches in Internet Archive; exact institutional catalog records; broader web, IIIF, and digital-gallery searches
evidence.summary: Exact Internet Archive searches for each shelfmark and title-plus-manuscript combinations returned zero items. The two BL records had blank Digitised Content fields, and no verified NLS digital object was exposed. Broader shelfmark/IIIF searches yielded no public full codex facsimile. This is a dated negative finding, not proof that no institutional photography, unindexed microfilm, or legacy surrogate exists.
notes: Ask BL and NLS specifically about unindexed institutional photography and legacy microfilm before concluding that reproduction must begin from the originals.

open_questions.question: Does U-M consider the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML superseded by the current No Copyright-United States label, and may the XML encoding and markup be redistributed in a commercial or nonprofit corpus?
next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library that separately addresses copyright status, provider terms, nonprofit redistribution, commercial redistribution, and reuse of the encoding and markup.
Until clarified, cite both the current NoC-US label and the embedded restriction rather than selecting whichever is more permissive.

open_questions.question: What is the exact relationship among Add MS 38690, Add MS 5467, and Adv.MS.17.1.22, and should the NLS copy be dated to the sixteenth or seventeenth century?
next_steps: Consult Matheson's full manuscript and stemmatic discussion and later paleographical work.
Ask NLS for the basis of its current sixteenth-century date and whether the hand has been reassessed since Neilson's seventeenth-century description.
Compare complete witness texts directly when full facsimiles or permitted reproductions become available; do not infer a direct copying relation beforehand.

open_questions.question: Do BL or NLS hold unindexed manuscript photography, legacy microfilm, or other digital surrogates for the three witnesses, and what exact research and reuse terms would apply?
next_steps: Ask BL reference services about Add MS 5467 and Add MS 38690, including any legacy microfilm or unindexed photography and the distinction between reference and reproduction images.
Ask NLS about existing photography or microfilm of Adv.MS.17.1.22 before ordering new images.
If ordering, specify exact folios, image resolution, color target, file format, delivery rights, publication rights, and whether images may be redistributed with the corpus.
Record supplied files and item-specific terms without generalizing from institution-wide copyright pages.

open_questions.question: Who composed the lost Latin narrative, where and when was it written, what exemplar did Shirley translate, and what was the translation's original recipient and purpose?
next_steps: Consult Matheson's full source, recipient, and treatment chapters and later scholarship.
Keep the source as an unidentified related work rather than assigning an author, Scottish official-publication history, or physical witness without direct evidence.
Keep circa 1440 as a traditional approximate translation date rather than an exact date proven by the 1818 title page.
